lines: Let's begin. Wherever you are, whatever you've been doing — pause. You've been given a few minutes to set things down. Not forever. Just for now.
Start with your breath. We'll use a slow pattern — a long exhale that tells your nervous system it's safe to stand down.
Breathe in through your nose for four.
Exhale slowly through your mouth for four.
And release... all the way out... three... four... five... six... seven.
Good. Let your breathing return to whatever rhythm it finds. Just notice how it feels to have slowed down, even for a moment. Your body knows the difference. Trust what it tells you.
Now listen to these words. They were spoken to people who were exhausted — not lazy, not undisciplined, not weak. Just... worn through. The way you might be right now.

"Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy and my burden is light."
Matthew eleven, verses twenty-eight through thirty.

You are carrying something right now. Maybe you can name it — a deadline, a relationship that's draining you, a decision that won't stop circling, a responsibility that feels too large for one person. Or maybe it's just a heaviness with no clear label. A tiredness that sleep doesn't fix. A low-grade exhaustion you've gotten so used to you've stopped noticing it.
Either way... you're invited to set it down. Not permanently. Not irresponsibly. Just here. Just for these few minutes.

And we're going to do that through your body. Because stress doesn't just live in your thoughts — it buries itself in your muscles. Your shoulders carry it. Your jaw holds it. Your stomach braces against it all day long. So let's go there.

We're going to use a practice called progressive muscle relaxation. The idea is simple: you deliberately tense a muscle group — hold it — and then release. The release teaches your body what letting go actually feels like, because most of the time, you've forgotten.
Start with your hands. Make fists. Squeeze them tight — really tight. Feel the tension in every finger, every knuckle, the muscles running up into your forearms. Feel what it costs to grip this hard.
Hold... five... four... three... two... one.
And release. Open your hands completely. Let them go soft. Let them fall open.
Feel the difference. That contrast — between gripping and letting go — that's what we're practicing with everything you're carrying. The grip is familiar. The release... you might have to relearn.
Now your arms. Tense your biceps, your forearms — make them rigid, like you're bracing to lift something impossibly heavy. Feel the effort. The strain.
Hold that... feel every tight fiber...
Hold... five... four... three... two... one.
And release. Let your arms go completely limp. Heavy. Useless. Like they're made of warm sand, slowly pouring out.
Notice what release actually feels like in the muscle. Not just the absence of tension — the warmth that follows. The softness. The quiet hum of a muscle that has remembered it's allowed to rest.

Your shoulders now. Pull them up toward your ears. High as they'll go. This is the posture of stress — the shape your body makes when it's carrying too much. When the world is too heavy and you're trying to hold it up with your trapezius muscles.
Hold it there. All the weight of the day gathered in your shoulders. All the things you're responsible for. All the plates you're spinning. Feel what it costs.
Hold... five... four... three... two... one.
And let them drop. All at once. Don't lower them carefully — just... drop.
Let gravity do the work. Your shoulders have nowhere to be but down. They've been up near your ears for hours, maybe days. Let them rest.
"For my yoke is easy and my burden is light."

Your face now. Scrunch everything — your forehead, your eyes, your nose, your mouth, your jaw. Tight. Like you're bracing for bad news. Like someone's about to tell you something you don't want to hear. This is what stress looks like in miniature, written across your face all day without you knowing.
Hold it... feel the strain behind your eyes, in your temples, along your jaw...
Hold... five... four... three... two... one.

And release. Let your forehead smooth. Let your eyes soften behind the lids. Let your jaw come slightly open — teeth apart, tongue resting. Let your whole face go slack and peaceful, like the face of someone sleeping.
Rest there. Feel the quiet in your face. The openness of a brow that's not furrowed.

Now your stomach. Tighten your core — pull everything in, like you're bracing for impact. This is what your body does when it's always waiting for the next hard thing. Constant bracing. Always prepared for a blow that may never come. It's exhausting — not the blow itself, but the waiting.
Hold that tension. Feel how tiring it is just to brace...
Hold... five... four... three... two... one.

And release. Let your belly be soft. Let it expand with your breath. Let it be round and unguarded. You don't need to brace right now. Nothing is coming at you in this moment. You are allowed to be undefended.
Let a breath fill your belly. Feel it push against your loosened muscles. Soft. Easy. This is how you were designed to breathe.
Your legs. Tense everything — your thighs, your calves, your feet. Push your heels into the floor. Hard. Like you're ready to run. Like your body is preparing for fight or flight.
Hold... feel the energy stored in those muscles, the readiness, the coiled urgency...
Hold... five... four... three... two... one.
And release. Let your legs go heavy. Let them sink into whatever's supporting them. Feel them become dead weight — gloriously, peacefully dead weight.
Good. Now... one more time. Your whole body at once. Tense everything. Fists, arms, shoulders, face, stomach, legs — all of it. Every muscle engaged. Every fiber holding on.
This is what carrying the burden feels like. All of it, all at once. The job, the family, the money, the health, the future, the past — all clenched in every muscle of your body.
Hold it... hold all of it...
And release everything. All at once. Like setting down an armful of stones. Let it all go.

Stay here. Don't rush past this. Don't immediately start thinking about what's next. Just... feel what your body is like when it's not holding anything. The looseness. The warmth. The extraordinary quiet of a body that has stopped fighting.
This is what it feels like to set things down.
"Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest."

This is not a suggestion for the strong. It's an invitation for the tired. For the person who's been holding everything together by sheer force of will and is starting to crack. For the one who says "I'm fine" forty times a day and isn't. For you. Right now. Tired as you are.

Let's do a second, gentler pass through the body. No more tensing this time — just attention. Just breath. We're going to move through each area again, and wherever you find residual tension, just breathe into it. No force. No effort. Just warmth and attention and permission.

Lord, this body is tired. Not just physically — though that too. Not just today's tired — a deeper tired. The kind that comes from carrying things I was never built to carry alone. From bracing against problems I can't solve by bracing. From holding everything together while quietly falling apart.

You said to come. You said you'd give rest. So I'm coming. Not with my life figured out. Not with my stress resolved. Not cleaned up or put together. Just... weary. And trusting that the invitation was real. That it's still real. That it was meant for someone exactly this tired.
Teach me to set things down more often. Not because the work doesn't matter, but because I matter too. And because you are strong enough — really, actually strong enough — to hold what I cannot.

Thank you for this body. For its ability to tense and to release. For the muscles that carried me today and the breath that keeps coming without my effort. For the heartbeat I never asked for and the rest you offer without condition.
I receive it now. Not because I've earned it. Because you've given it.
When you're ready, begin to bring your attention back. Wiggle your fingers, slowly. Move your toes. Take a deep breath — a full one, filling your lungs all the way up.
Feel the surface beneath you. The temperature of the air. The sounds of wherever you are. The world is still here. It didn't fall apart while you rested.

You can carry things differently now. Not because the weight has changed, but because you've remembered that you're allowed to set it down. That you were always allowed. That the invitation stands — today, tomorrow, the next time the tension starts to build.

Move gently into whatever comes next. And when you feel the bracing start again — the shoulders creeping up, the jaw tightening, the stomach clenching — remember: your body knows how to release. You just practiced. You can come back to this. As many times as you need.
